data.title: Not Without Risk: Why The BoC Is Signaling A Future Rate Cut Is Possible
data.description:
The Bank of Canada has held its key rate, but what would lead it to cut? MoneyTalk discusses with Andrew Kelvin, Head of Canadian and Global Rates Strategy with TD Securities.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell - The Bank of Canada has once again held its key overnight rate. Governor Tiff Macklem is having to balance concerns about slowing economic growth with continued stickiness in inflation. Joining us now to discuss is Andrew Kelvin, Managing Director and Head of Canadian and Global Rate Strategy with TD Securities. Andrew, great to have you back on the program.

Andrew Kelvin - Thank you for having me. Always a pleasure.

Greg Bonnell - All right. Another rate decision. We're back here together again. Another hold after a streak of, what was it, seven cuts? What's going on? What's keeping them on hold?

Andrew Kelvin - So the June meeting was really a follow-through on the April meeting. In fact, if you look at their communiqué, their second to last paragraph, which is usually the forward-looking part of it, they usually end with some sort of vague statement and platitudes about our mission is to promote price stability or what have you, which is their mission. But we always go to the very bottom of the communiqués when they come out to see what they said in their forward looking component.

This time, they copy pasted it from April unchanged, which I think makes sense. Because I think from the Bank of Canada's perspective, not much has changed from April. When we were trying to put together our preview and collect our thoughts, one of the things we looked at is all the changes in the global trade front that had occurred.

And we'd gone from significant global trade frictions, to détente, to more trade frictions, to détente again. And then the cherry on top, after we'd already put our preview out, was an increase in steel and aluminum tariffs. And the Bank actually referred to that, and they just said, look, there's a lot of uncertainty here.

They can't be as forward looking as they previously had been. What that always implied was that they were going to need to really be a bit more reactive on the data as it came in. And if you look at the last, certainly, six or seven weeks of Canadian data, you're not going to find a clear signal on which way the economy is going.

So, absent a clear signal, the Bank of Canada's decision was to stay on hold, which I think is sensible, particularly given that it's a July 30 meeting next time. So it's quite late in July after being quite early in June. We will have quite a bit more data when they meet again in July. And, presumably, we'll have a bit more clarity as to what the global trade picture looks like as well, as well as what the uncertainty impacts are having in Canada.

Greg Bonnell - Well, as you dig into the statement, obviously, we just got that GDP report was stronger than expected. Some people may have said-- the casual observer may have said, but wasn't April inflation like at 1.7% below the Bank's target? Bank seems to be looking past that. They still seem to be concerned about inflation.

Andrew Kelvin - So that was, I think, to the extent there was something new we learned in this meeting. It wasn't so much that they're concerned about inflation, because we always know that the Bank of Canada is concerned about inflation. They are an inflation targeting central bank.

I think the thing I was struck by was that there may not be quite as much leeway in terms of how much inflation surprises they're willing to tolerate going forward. Now, headline inflation, you're right, it's below 2%. But that was on the consumer carbon tax being set to 0. That's a very easy shock to look through.

You know precisely why inflation fell that 6/10 of a percentage point. You know it will be in the inflation data for precisely 12 months. And you know it doesn't really reflect the underlying inflationary dynamic. And the same was true when carbon taxes were increasing.

The Bank would routinely look through those incremental tenth of a percentage point because that wasn't something they could control and not really something they should be trying to control here. So they're looking at the broader underlying price pressures. They're seeing that those are a little bit firmer than anticipated.

And I think it's important to note that, without knowing the result of the election, which was the case when the Bank of Canada came in April, both the leading parties, both the presumptive governing parties, were promising to set the consumer carbon tax to 0.

So that was already in their forecast. That was no surprise to them. And the fact that we still had an upside surprise on CPI relative to the previous forecasts, it did suggest to them that maybe inflation is a little bit firmer than they anticipated. And the thing I always go back to-- policymakers in general, global policymakers, they're always fighting the last battle.

The last thing a policymaker wants to do is not make the same mistake twice. You don't want to step on a rake and then immediately step on that very same rake.

Greg Bonnell - Saw a show like that once.

Andrew Kelvin - Yeah. Quite amusing.

So, for the Bank of Canada, the previous rake they stepped on was leaving policy rates too low in late 2021 and early 2022. I think with the benefit of hindsight, they probably would have started tightening in July 2021. And I think even without hindsight, there's a case for them to be tightening as early as October 2021.

And, as it stands, inflation was high for a longer period than they anticipated. Yes, some of it was global supply chain factors. Some of it was global demand due to global fiscal. But, certainly, the central bank probably could have done more with the benefit of hindsight.

Having gone through an inflationary episode, gotten inflation back down to about 2%, the last thing they want to do is cut too much in front of shocks that may not materialize and produce another inflationary spike. That is how inflation expectations ultimately become unanchored. So that's what they're trying to avoid here.

And I think if there's a message I got from Governor Macklem today that was a new message-- the governor was asked-- I'm going to paraphrase a bit here-- but the governor was asked if inflation needed to move lower in the next two months for there to be a cut in July.

Now, he's never going to answer these questions, yes or no. The one thing the Bank of Canada has come to understand is optionality is their friend. They never want to paint themselves into a corner.

Greg Bonnell - Never say something, if you're buying a house, starting a business, getting a big loan, don't worry, rates will be low for a long time-- something like that.

Andrew Kelvin - Yeah, something like that that hems them in has been, in the past, not something that's worked out especially well for them. So I think they've done a really good job in this cycle about being very forthright about what they know, what they don't know, and trying to keep options on the table for as long as possible.

So when the governor is asked if inflation needs to come lower to cut rates, he's never going to be like, yes, or he's never going to say no because there's more to it, then there's so many moving parts. They don't know what the things outside the very narrow inflation data that could happen in the next eight weeks, because just look at the course of 2025.

So much of what's happened was unpredictable that they, again, don't want to get pinned in the corner. Having said all that-- I'm taking a long time to get to this point, I recognize that. There is a point, I promise.

When he was asked about it, rather than dismissing it and saying it will depend on a whole host of factors, he did dive into a fairly long discussion about how inflation has firmed to a greater extent than they expected. They talked about ex tax inflation, how their core measures were a bit firmer than they had been a few months previously. It was a long explanation. And while he didn't give a yes/no, it felt decisively like not a no, to put it another way.

Greg Bonnell - There's a lot of uncertainty, obviously, with our largest trading partner. We do have the certainty now as to who has formed the government-- Prime Minister Mark Carney and the Liberals. But we're not going to budget. So what does the Bank of Canada make of that?

Do they need some fiscal clarity? We have promises about Canada being a energy superpower, about new construction of infrastructure. But what does the Bank do with that information until they see a budget?

Andrew Kelvin - So the governor was asked this. And his line, which I think is appropriate, was that uncertainty around Canadian fiscal policy is not the biggest source of uncertainty the Bank of Canada is facing here.

Greg Bonnell - It's not what keeps them awake at night?

Andrew Kelvin - It's not what's keeping them awake at night. The Bank has a very simple process here. They will put fiscal measures into their projections once those measures are announced and legislated.

They are not going to start trying to anticipate the changes a new government will make. And when you talk about things like making Canada an energy superpower, these are not overnight changes. These are, perhaps, not changes that can occur within a monetary policy window, which is typically thought of as six to eight quarters, or even a single electoral mandate.

Having said all that, if you look at the Liberal platform, there are some measures in it that will have more short-term economic impacts, tax cuts front of mind for that. Once those are enacted and start to flow through into the economy, the bank will reflect that in their forecasts. So when I think about what the later budget does to the way we're thinking about the Bank of Canada path, you have this window in the summer where the House is no longer in session, we, presumably, haven't seen a budget.

I've seen no indication that we're going to have a budget rushed in before the House rises for the summer. And you have this period where you haven't seen significant new fiscal stimulus into the forecast. Now, the need for it now is probably a little bit less than what we would have feared. The government did acknowledge today that the economy seems to veering more towards the more benign scenario that they'd imagined, rather than the more severe one.

But what that does is it opens up a period where we have two more quarters where the economy is going to be mostly operating under the influence of external factors-- under the impacts of what the trade tensions are going to be doing, the changing demographics, the monetary stimulus already in the system. It's an economy that's not going to be getting that boost from the government for the next few quarters.

That's the most likely window for the BOC to be able to cut. If we get to the October meeting and we're still having this conversation about how they're waiting for weakness to show up, I think a reasonable conclusion is that the weakness isn't going to be coming to the same extent that, perhaps, we had feared. So when I think about this sort of probabilistically, I look at Q3 as a period where rate cuts are far more plausible than, let's say, the first quarter of 2026.

Greg Bonnell - When do they start? Quickly, I'll ask you-- what do you got baked in the forecast right now? When do they get off the sidelines again?

Andrew Kelvin - So we have three more cuts on our forecast. We have cuts in July, September, and October. By the time you get to December, I think it should be reasonably apparent what the damage done from trade uncertainty is. I'm sure there will be some.

The magnitude is really hard to determine, but I think by December, whatever the economy looks like due to those trade tensions, it should be mostly reflected in activity. And by the time we hit that December meeting, you're now getting that Q4 data. You now have an idea of what is going to be coming on the fiscal policy side of things. And we will have three more cuts in our forecast, so you'll have moved from a neutral policy setting to a slightly accommodative one.

At that point, we don't think anything more will be needed. And it really just comes down to a question of, how can the Canadian economy weather things through the summer? We see, essentially, zero growth over Q2 and Q3. If I want to put my optimist hat on, I can find some reasons to think that maybe there will be things coming that we, perhaps, do not anticipate.

A really interesting conversation for me, which I think probably a lot of our viewers are having themselves, is around what happens with vacation spending. We have seen these indications that Canadians traveling to the US, those numbers have started to fall off a little bit. I'm not a huge believer in this notion that Canadians can wholesale replace their imported consumption baskets with domestic ones.

We import 30% to 35% of GDP. I think it's 32%, 33% of GDP. To replace that with domestic sources is probably not plausible. But if there's one big ticket item that Canadians do tend to spend abroad and can be easily substituted domestically, it is travel.

Some of that travel spending that had previously gone to the US will be saved. It just won't wind up in the economy. Some of it will be rerouted to other non-Canadian but non-US destinations. Some of it will go to the US. If you've promised you're taking your kids to Disney World, you may well still be taking your kids to Disney World. There's not a good substitute for that domestically.

But some of that will be redirected domestically. There will be Canadians who, perhaps, have been thinking about going to the US, but given that the currency is-- well, Canadian dollar has appreciated a little bit. The exchange rate is still not especially favorable for Canadian travelers, given that maybe there is some sentiment changes in terms of a buy Canada theme and buy Canada narrative-- we could see a little bit more domestic tourism this summer, which would be an upside risk to our forecast.

All told, we have zero growth. If that materializes, we think we can get three more cuts. If it looks a little bit better than that, the case for cutting is just simply not as compelling.

Original Post

data.title: Tandem Diabetes Care: A Barbell Bet On A High-Stakes Turnaround
data.description:
Introduction

My last look at Tandem Diabetes Care, Inc. (NASDAQ:TNDM) was nearly two years ago today. The medical device company known for its automated insulin delivery (AID) systems was struggling to begin 2023, mired by declining pump shipments, narrowing gross profits, and widening net losses. Still, Tandem was hopeful for 10%-plus growth and international expansion. My recommendation was a “cautious Hold.” Its stock has since declined 22.68% while the S&P 500 has rallied 36.73%.

The following article reveals a mixed financial performance for Tandem, but there are reasons to hold for risk-tolerant investors.

Financials

Upon a glance at Tandem’s financial data, it’s plain to see why its stock has underperformed. Revenues tanked nearly 7% in 2023, far from its optimistic estimates. Notably, the company did turn things around in 2024, growing revenues 25.7% year-over-year to reach an all-time high of $940 million. However, its 2024 performance did not rally its stock. Although its Q4 2024 earnings report featured notable beats on EPS ($0.01) and revenue ($282.6 million), its 2025 guidance worried the market. Tandem guided for between $997 million and $1.007 billion in revenues, which appeared soft to analysts.

Fast-forward to Q1 2025, Tandem generated $234.4 million in worldwide sales, compared to $191.67 million in Q1 2024. Pump shipments in the U.S. increased by 19%. However, there were a few financial events that contributed to a widening net loss of $130.6 million, including $75.217 million in acquired in-process R&D expenses, $6.697 million in non-recurring facility impairment costs, and $4.47 million in restructuring costs.

Notably, there were meaningful improvements in gross profit and Adjusted EBITDA relative to last year. Tandem reaffirmed its 2025 guidance.

Recent Developments

In the AID system market, Tandem’s Control-IQ+ competes with Medtronic plc's (MDT) MiniMed 780G, Insulet’s Omnipod 5, Beta Bionics iLet Bionic Pancreas, and Sequel’s twiist. The latter was introduced last year. Each system has its idiosyncrasies, and no one is “better” than the other. The core functionalities are similar. Tandem’s unique feature is that it has a predictive algorithm and an extended bolus of up to eight hours. Insulet is tubeless. And so on.

The problem is that Tandem is tasked with competing with healthcare conglomerates like Medtronic. So, it’s challenging for smaller players like Tandem to balance heavy innovation investments with limited funding. Tandem has no option but to take on debt.

While we’re on the subject, as of March 31, Tandem had $368.6 million in cash and short-term investments. It has $40.7 million in “current portion of long-term debt.” This is debt that is due within the next 12 months. Tandem’s long-term debt is $313.6 million. In Q1, Tandem “burned” $18.3 million (cash from operations). Assuming a similar cash burn rate moving forward, Tandem’s cash and investments afford it approximately five years of cash runway. However, this doesn’t account for Tandem’s upcoming debt obligations. Most of Tandem’s convertible senior notes are due in 2029. So, Tandem has time to sort things out, and it believes it can achieve free cash flow positivity in the second half of 2025.

On the innovation front, Tandem’s AID is undergoing a few changes. It intends to get rid of the tubing in the Mobi platform. The algorithm is evolving, and Tandem hopes to achieve fully closed-loop technology. Extended wear infusion technology aims to provide longer-lasting infusion sets. There are connectivity improvements, like expanding smartphone control capabilities. This is all very tedious, but it’s required to stay ahead in a market characterized by many similar products. Many of these improvements target convenience.

Tandem’s Control-IQ+ was approved for type 2 diabetes in February 2025, which more than doubles its addressable market. For those who chose the computer class over anatomy and physiology in high school, most patients with type 2 diabetes still produce their insulin and, thus, don’t require exogenous insulin from an AID. However, it has been estimated that 12.3% of diabetes patients need exogenous insulin within a year of diagnosis. So, Tandem will focus on patients with a high insulin burden. Tandem also remains focused on international expansion. In Q1, international sales grew 35% year-over-year.

Valuation Analysis and Sensitivity

I conducted a discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis on TNDM. The purpose of doing this is to play “What If?” The company’s long-term financial goals are 25% operating margins and 60% gross margins by 2026. So, let’s assume the company achieves cash flow positivity and maintains revenue growth at 10%, on average, over the nine-year projection period.

In such a case, TNDM is undervalued by 38.25%. However, be aware that the above projection is an “assumption within an assumption.” Although the company believes it can generate cash, it has yet to prove this.

Tandem is highly sensitive to growth and margin assumptions.

The sensitivity analysis that follows centers on our Base scenario.

TNDM is a stock with substantial upside if management executes on its goals. But this is a big "if," and the upside is not asymmetric. The company has a long history of operating losses. Its operating expenses remain high. There is intense competition. On the other hand, it is growing revenue at a strong pace. Gross margins are improving.

Conclusion

To wrap up, Tandem is seeing financial improvements, but the market remains very cautious. And I cannot blame them. While there is considerable upside should Tandem exceed conservative expectations, there is also substantial downside (90%-plus) should companies like Medtronic continue to eat Tandem’s lunch. So, TNDM only makes sense in a barbell portfolio, where allocation is limited to offset the potential for substantial losses (maintain a Hold rating). With that said, a turnaround could be near (the achievement of consistent free cash flow), and TNDM is worth watching.

data.title: Currency Coordination Looks Riskier Today
data.description:
By Victor Xing

The Taiwan dollar’s rapid appreciation in the second quarter led to speculation of a “Plaza Accord 2.0” — a coordinated effort to weaken the US dollar — echoing the historic 1985 agreement among G5 nations. The original Plaza Accord was designed to address large US trade deficits by engineering a controlled depreciation of the dollar through joint currency intervention. It marked a rare and powerful example of global currency coordination.

Any new Plaza-style agreement today would face far greater financial and geopolitical hurdles than it did 40 years ago. Indeed, if US policymakers seek to stimulate domestic manufacturing by depreciating the dollar, they must also account for the emerging costs and risks associated with global trade, capital flows, and market stability.

This post examines the potential consequences of a coordinated dollar depreciation today — from FX volatility and insurance risk to broader macroeconomic impacts.

A Weaker Dollar Could Heighten Global FX Volatility

A weaker US dollar could have a dramatic effect on the FX market and, specifically, on Taiwanese life insurance companies. A January 2025 FT article pointed out that these companies hold assets equivalent to 140% of Taiwan’s GDP. A substantial portion of these holdings are in US-dollar-denominated bonds only partially hedged for FX volatility.

Taiwan has enjoyed widening current account surpluses due in large part to strong demand for its semiconductors. To manage the resulting FX reserve growth and to maintain FX stability, the local monetary authority permitted life insurance companies to swap their Taiwan dollars for US dollars in the FX reserve. The insurers then swapped USD to buy US fixed-income assets to meet future (insurance policy) obligations.

Despite shifting the bulk of their portfolio assets to US dollars, most of the insurance policies (firm liabilities) remain denominated in local currency. The result would be a significant currency mismatch where sharp declines in the US dollar would reduce the value of US-dollar-denominated bonds such as US Treasuries held by Taiwanese insurance companies, leaving the insurance companies with insufficient assets to match their liabilities.

The original Plaza Accord signed by the G-5 countries in 1985 was agreed upon under the backdrop of a relatively benign macro environment. A hypothetical “Plaza Accord 2.0” to depreciate the US dollar would likely increase pressure on Taiwan’s insurers and their risk-management efforts. This vicious cycle would exacerbate pressure and magnify FX market volatility.

Taiwanese insurance companies are also exposed to duration risks. The US dollar bonds held by Taiwanese insurance companies are longer-duration (with greater interest rate sensitivity than short-maturity debt). Sales of these assets would likely lift long-term US interest rates and transmit interest rate volatility across markets.

Taiwanese insurers are not alone in their exposure to this type of risk. Similar carry-trade flows (sell local currency, buy US dollar and dollar-denominated assets) with the Japanese yen in the third quarter of 2024 triggered a brief-but-disruptive volatility surge across major asset markets.

The US Trade Deficit’s Hidden Role

A “Plaza Accord 2.0” coming 40 years after the original accord would need to account for the US trade deficit as part of a circular currency flow to fund the US government. In 1985, the US deficit was at $211.9 billion. By 2024 it had risen to $1.8 trillion. Similarly, the US debt ballooned from $1.8 trillion in 1985 to $36.2 trillion in the second quarter this year. Non-US exporters reinvesting trade surplus dollars in US Treasuries (lending surplus dollars back to the US government) are a key source of liquidity in the US bond market:

Under the present paradigm, a lower US trade deficit would likely disrupt the reinvestment of exporter dollar trade surpluses, which could reduce foreign demand at US Treasury auctions and negatively affect secondary market liquidity conditions.

“Plaza Accord 2.0’s” Nuanced Impact On a Leaner US Manufacturing Sector

The US manufacturing sector has evolved significantly over the past 40 years. According to BEA data, the US manufacturing sector’s share of nominal GDP fell to 9.9% in 4Q 2024 from 18.5% in 1985.The total number of workers in the manufacturing sector also declined. In April 1985, manufacturing employees as a share of total non-farm payrolls was 18.4%. By April 2025, that number had dropped to 8.0%. The reduction in manufacturing headcount (with improved productivity, until gains began to stagnate in the late 2000s) implies US manufacturing had become more efficient between 1987 and 2007:

Thus, a changed manufacturing industry with relatively smaller payrolls now than in 1985 would likely benefit differently from impacts of Plaza style accords than four decades ago, when more households were directly participating in the industry.

Assessing the Risk Reward of “Plaza Accord 2.0”

Studies on the impact of the original Plaza Accord concluded that exchange rate shifts ultimately led to changes in trade balances with a lag of two years. A similar lag would likely apply today, raising questions about whether a new Plaza-style intervention could meaningfully support US manufacturing — now a leaner, smaller share of GDP — without triggering broader financial disruptions. Compared to 1985, today’s global system is more interconnected and more reliant on the dollar, particularly through foreign holdings of US debt. Any coordinated effort to weaken the dollar would need to balance potential industrial gains against risks to FX stability, institutional asset-liability mismatches, and the functioning of US debt markets. The cost-benefit equation for “Plaza Accord 2.0” is far more complex than it was four decades ago.

Calls for a “Plaza Accord 2.0” reflect growing concern over US trade imbalances and industrial competitiveness. But unlike in 1985, the global economy today is more complex, with deeper interdependencies and more fragile financial linkages. A new Plaza-style agreement would carry unintended consequences — from FX volatility and insurance-sector risk in Asia to disruptions in US debt financing and monetary policy transmission.

Under the original Plaza Accord, currency shifts took years to influence trade balances, underscoring the lag between intervention and impact. Policymakers must therefore assess whether the benefits to a leaner US manufacturing base would outweigh the risks to global markets, institutional stability, and US fiscal operations. In this environment, the risk-reward calculus of currency coordination looks far more complicated than it did 40 years ago.

Disclaimer: Please note that the content of this site should not be construed as investment advice, nor do the opinions expressed necessarily reflect the views of CFA Institute.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Energy Transfer Is Your Perfect AI Bet
data.description:
My Coverage History

So far, I've written 2 articles on Energy Transfer LP Common Units (NYSE:ET), both with a "Buy rating. My first one came out in early November 2024, calling to buy the stock before its likely Q3 earnings beat. The firm didn't beat back then, but the unit price kept its rally until early 2025 when ET went through a correction. In mid-March 2025, I decided to reiterate my "Buy" on ET, arguing that its extensive natural gas pipeline network and rising natural gas power demand "positioned it well for future growth and stable DCF", so ET presented a perfect "buy the dip" opportunity at the time. It's been almost 3 months since that call of mine, but my thesis hasn't aged well so far—as the S&P 500 index (SP500) (SPX) kept recovering, the ET unit's comeback was too slow.

My Updated Thesis on ET

Despite Energy Transfer's underperformance compared to SPY's price action in recent weeks, I believe that, as surprising as it might sound, the ET units are great for those looking for AI exposure at an attractive price. I believe Energy Transfer's solutions will be in massive demand in the next few years, as it's positioning itself to capitalize on one of the most significant secular growth trends of our time. The valuation of ET is too attractive to ignore, in my opinion, so I keep my "Buy" in place.

My Updated Reasoning

From ET's Q1 FY2025 report, which came out in early May this year, we see an adjusted EBITDA of $4.1 billion, up by about 5.1% on a YoY basis, which is quite remarkable given that the top line (revenues) came in at only $21.02 billion (-2.82% YoY), slightly missing the consensus estimate. The EPS figure of $0.36 was up 12.5% YoY, but what's more important for unit holders, the DCF (distributable cash flow), which defines the units' payout, was $2.3 billion, only slightly less (by ~2%) than last year.

Without focusing too much on the segments' performance, which was already well-covered in other post-earnings articles on Seeking Alpha, I'd like to jump straight to the growth opportunity I see in front of Energy Transfer in the next few years at least.

As you might know, the recent surge in AI technology development is going hand in hand with the increasing need for energy generation.

AI will be the most significant driver of this increase, with electricity demand from AI-optimised data centres projected to more than quadruple by 2030. In the United States, power consumption by data centres is on course to account for almost half of the growth in electricity demand between now and 2030.

Source: IEA

That's why we utilities stocks (XLU) outgrow both SPY and tech stocks (XLK) in the 12-month time frame, which is a real rarity in recent years; those who generate and sell energy become "shovel sellers", so the well-known proverb calls on institutional buyers to action.

That's why Energy Transfer's weakness is what I didn't expect to see; although ET managed to beat SPY in the same 12-month time frame, its dip from the January highs looks overdone.

Energy Transfer seems to be correctly positioning itself to capitalize on the rising need for AI capacity expansion. The Co-CEO, Tom Long, stated on the earnings call that they are in advanced discussions with several other facilities near their footprint "to supply store and transport natural gas from gas-fired power plants, data centers, and industrial and onshore manufacturing". So their agreement with CloudBurst data centers "to provide natural gas to their flagship AI-focused data center development in Central Texas" is just the tip of the iceberg, as there are "150 approximate data centers that ET's looking at just in Texas alone", according to the Co-CEO's commentary, Mackie McCrea.

There are 150 approximate data centers that we're looking at just in Texas alone, we would love to elaborate and talk about some significant progress we make. We do believe in the next four to six weeks, we'll be able to make some significant announcements.

It's been 4 weeks since that earnings call, and so we're heading right to the heart of the "4-6 weeks" window.

The unique positioning of ET's assets is their proximity to existing infrastructure, so building out new projects around it usually requires relatively low capital and revenue generation can be initiated quickly. I think natural gas will be the go-to fuel solution for reliable and scalable power for these energy-hungry AI facilities because a) there's no real alternative available today, and b) some possible alternatives, such as nuclear energy, would take a few years to be operational and functional. The assets network that Energy Transfer has today is a clear competitive advantage that is very likely to support DCF generation for years to come, in my view, even if crude oil prices keep sliding lower.

Beyond AI, ET's offerings are also supported by a nice and wide pipeline of projects, some of which are in late status (meaning they'll go active shortly). For example, their Lake Charles LNG project is projected to get an FID (final investment decision) by the year's end, and the binding SPA with a Japanese utility for up to 1 MTPA and an HOA with a German energy company for another 1 MTPA would only add diversification benefit and international opportunity.

As the processed tonnage for Lake Charles LNG offtake grows, and the AI opportunity I described above materializes, I'd expect continued growth in ET's distributions.

The Street expects $1.38 per unit for FY2026, and admitting that the nature of Energy Transfer's operations requires a heightened discount rate (I'll take 10%), a classical DDM model shows that ET is currently undervalued by over 57%:

But even at a 3% growth rate, the current unit price looks too cheap to sustain:

Most valuation multiples agree with the above findings that ET might be undervalued by 12-50%:

Given all that, I'm reiterating my "Buy" on ET units today.

Risks And Your Takeaway

As I noted in my most recent update on ET, one key area of risk for the company is coming from its commodity market dependence. I mean, while being quite well-diversified in a variety of operations and end markets, the unit price will likely stay volatile depending on the prices of NG, crude oil, NGLs, or shocks in those source markets. Also, some regulations could create headwinds to ET's top line and result in earnings drops and lower DCFs in the future. Thus, my valuation calculations may turn out to be off by a mile.

Despite the above risks, I think that ET can mitigate commodity price fluctuations with fee-based contracts (~90% of expected margin), and the existing risks concerning execution or regulatory headwinds touch all firms in the niche, so I think ET's track record of past execution is what we should prioritize in figuring out its risk profile.

Anyway, the AI opportunity is there, in my view, and it's only getting clearer quarter by quarter as the need for efficient energy generation expands. Natural gas solutions are the key to future reliable energy generation, and ET's well-interconnected pipeline network and substantial storage capabilities make it an ideal partner for this rapidly expanding industry, in my understanding. If so, I'd bet that ET's DCF will keep growing at a higher-than-average growth rate in the next few years. At least the 3% growth rate is what I'd place as a base case scenario; this gives me an undervaluation of 12.5% plus the distribution yield of 7-8% annually (at the current price).

So, I'm still bullish on Energy Transfer after its dip.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: WTI Crude, Brent Crude Forecast: WTI Rally On Pause At Key Resistance Level
data.description:
By Christian Norman

Trading some ~12.80% higher than 24-month lows made courtesy of Trump’s ‘Liberation Day’, WTI crude and Brent crude currently trade at ~$63.90 and ~$66.01, respectively.

WTI Crude (WTICOUSD) Price Analysis:

  • WTI crude is trading higher in today’s session, with the 20 and 50-period SMAs offering support. If price can maintain highs, bulls will aim towards the 100-period SMA, which has proven an area of significant resistance in recent memory.
  • In the short term, $62.63 is offered as the nearest level of support, while resistance lies at $63.60, intersecting with the trendline. The next few days of price action are particularly important for short-term oil performance, should price breakdown below the trendline or break above
  • According to the 14-period RSI, recent bullish momentum has grounds for a further leg up, still trading well below oversold levels

Brent Crude (BCOUSD) Price Analysis:

  • Unlike its American counterpart, Brent crude still looks to surpass the 50-period SMA, with the next few days of price action being critical for establishing support
  • In the short term, we can expect $64.20 to be the next level of resistance, with the same level being used as a period of support mid-April. Otherwise, if prices fall to $64.83, at least some buyers will return to the market
  • Similarly to WTI, and owing to sideways movement through much of May, the 14-period ADX rates the last two weeks as ‘weak or absent’ in trend strength. Price currently faces a ‘make-or-break’ scenario on approach to the trendline

Original Post

data.title: U.S. And Global Money Supply Surges To Record Highs
data.description:
By Jesse Colombo

After a brief contraction in 2022 & 2023, the U.S. M2 money supply is once again surging—rising by $1.2 trillion in just over a year. This renewed expansion is a major force behind gold's bull market.

Over the past year, gold has been on a powerful run—surging from around $2,100 to $3,400, a remarkable 62% gain. That’s especially impressive given that gold is not a speculative stock but a traditionally stable, conservative asset.

While precious metals investors have plenty to celebrate, many may not fully grasp what’s driving this bull market. Several forces are at play—such as aggressive gold accumulation by emerging-market central banks and the relentless rise in global debt.

Today, I want to spotlight one particularly important catalyst: the renewed surge in U.S. money supply growth, following a rare contraction in 2022 and 2023.

For those unfamiliar, the money supply refers to the total amount of currency units in circulation—whether U.S. dollars, British pounds, or Japanese yen. Tracking the money supply is essential for understanding the effects of monetary policy and assessing inflationary pressures, especially when the supply is expanding rapidly.

In the U.S., the two most commonly followed measures are M1 and M2. While they differ in composition and absolute value, they generally move in tandem and offer similar insights on a relative basis.

The chart below shows the U.S. M2 money supply—a broad measure that includes cash in circulation, checkable deposits, savings accounts, money market mutual funds, and small-denomination time deposits such as CDs. During the COVID pandemic in 2020 and 2021, M2 surged by roughly $6 trillion as the Federal Reserve unleashed massive stimulus measures to support the economy. However, in 2022 and 2023, the Fed reversed course slightly, leading to a rare and brief contraction in the money supply.

Since late 2023, however, the U.S. M2 money supply has resumed its upward trajectory, expanding by $1.2 trillion in just over a year. This renewed surge is one of the key drivers behind gold’s powerful bull market during that time. It underscores a broader truth: the long-term direction of the money supply is relentlessly upward, with pullbacks being both rare and short-lived.

The latest surge in the money supply is just a small piece of a much larger trend spanning the past century. From 1920 to 2025, the U.S. M1 money supply—which includes primarily cash in circulation and checkable deposits—has skyrocketed by an astonishing 86,000%. And, sadly, this long-term upward trajectory will not slow or stop anytime soon.

I’ve chosen to display the M1 chart here because M2 data doesn’t extend as far back, but the long-term path of M2 mirrors that of M1 closely—what matters most is the direction, and it’s unmistakably higher.

I’m sure you have all heard stories—or actually lived it yourself—but back in the mid-twentieth century, families were able to live comfortable lives on one income while the mother stayed home. They were able to own homes, have large families, save money for their kids’ college, and put away savings for retirement.

Unfortunately, as we all know, life isn’t like that anymore—often not even with two working parents who both have college degrees. What’s the reason?

The reason is inflation, which is the continued growth of the money supply. This has the effect of watering down the currency and reducing its purchasing power. It became much worse after the world’s major currencies were no longer backed by gold starting in 1971. For this reason, it’s important for everyone to understand the significance of the money supply and to monitor it as closely as we do stock averages like the Dow or S&P 500.

As I mentioned earlier, one of the key drivers behind gold’s surge over the past year was the renewed expansion of the U.S. money supply, which began in late 2023 following a brief two-year contraction. Now, I want to show you just how sensitive gold is to money supply growth. The chart below compares gold to the U.S. M2 money supply over the long term, and the correlation is clear: as the money supply rises, so does gold.

This strong relationship is why gold remains one of the most effective ways to preserve your hard-earned wealth against the erosion caused by monetary expansion and inflation, which are two sides of the same coin.

And lest you think this is just a U.S. issue—it's not. This is a truly global problem. Since 1971, virtually all of the world’s major currencies have been unbacked by gold, meaning there is no constraint on money supply expansion.

To confirm this, just look at the chart below, which shows global M2 money supply. It has soared by an astonishing $73.74 trillion—an increase of nearly 200%—since 2007.

Just as gold closely tracks the U.S. M2 money supply, it also moves in step with the global M2 money supply. That’s why gold serves as a valuable hedge against currency debasement—not just in the U.S., but worldwide.

To learn more about this dynamic, I encourage you to read my recent report titled Gold Isn’t Going Up—Your Money Is Just Losing Value.

The U.S. money supply contraction in 2022 and 2023 was largely driven by the Federal Reserve’s quantitative tightening (QT) policy during that period. This was an attempt to rein in some of the excesses created by the aggressive quantitative easing (QE) measures implemented in 2020 and 2021 during the pandemic.

The impact of this shift can be clearly seen in the chart below, which shows the Federal Reserve’s balance sheet—specifically the assets, such as various bonds, that the Fed has purchased and currently holds.

I believe another dramatic expansion in the U.S. money supply is not far off. Quantitative tightening tends to expose the fragility of our heavily indebted, stimulus-dependent economy—often triggering recessions or financial crises.

Given how deeply this addiction to easy money has taken hold since 2008, it’s only a matter of time before the Fed is forced to cut rates and return to quantitative easing—essentially, digital money printing. When that happens, it will be extremely bullish for gold and silver.

To summarize, I want to highlight that both the U.S. and global money supply are expanding once again after a rare two-year pause. This is a major—but overlooked—driver of the current precious metals bull market. And I don’t see this trend reversing anytime soon.

On the contrary, I expect it to accelerate as the next recession forces the Fed and other central banks to fire up the digital printing presses once again. If our money were still backed by gold, we wouldn’t be having this discussion.

Thankfully, we can still protect our hard-earned wealth from the corrosive effects of inflation by owning gold and silver.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Virgin Galactic: Bleak Prospects, But A Glimmer Of Hope
data.description:
Virgin Galactic's (NYSE:SPCE) stock has continued to struggle since I last suggested that raising capital wasn't really feasible due to the company's low market capitalization, making it difficult for Virgin Galactic to remain a going concern. The stock temporarily bounced higher after its first quarter earnings though, which seems to have been driven by investors reassessing the probability of Virgin Galactic transitioning to profitability before it runs out of cash.

I continue to believe that meaningful revenue is still at least 18 months away and that Virgin Galactic will continue to burn cash for some time after it begins ramping its operations. This situation is exacerbated by a debt maturity date that is drawing nearer. As a result, Virgin Galactic will likely need to rely on customer deposits to finance its business, which could provide it with several hundred million dollars cash.

While I continue to believe that Virgin Galactic's future is bleak, a shift in sentiment could make it easier for the company to raise capital or collect customer deposits, potentially creating a positive feedback loop. This makes the company a poor short candidate, even though I expect its share price to continue declining.

Virgin Galactic Business Updates

Virgin Galactic wants to perform its first glide test in the spring of 2026. Its first research spaceflight is expected to occur in the summer of 2026, followed by the first private astronaut flight in the fall. The company will be hard-pressed to meet these targets though, and any teething issues with the new Delta class ships could prove fatal due to its weak balance sheet.

The Delta class ship will provide the same experience as Unity but require less maintenance, enabling weekly flights. It is also larger and will transport six passengers instead of four. Virgin Galactic is targeting the production of two Delta ships in order to achieve breakeven. The full incremental cost of which is expected to be $50-$60 million per ship going forward. Virgin Galactic expects its spaceships to last upwards of 500 flights, with most of the components of the propulsion system expected to last the life of the ship. This suggests that the cost of these ships is fairly modest compared to the revenue they are expected to generate.

Even if these ships are successfully built within the next 12 months, there will still be questions about Virgin Galactic's ability to perform flights at a high frequency though. Virgin Galactic is targeting 125 flights per year, which is around one flight every three days. A faster turnaround time is supported by the ability to rapidly swap the ship's rocket motor. The use of off-the-shelf avionics hardware along with proprietary software is expected to reduce maintenance needs. A step change in performance is required though, given that Virgin Galactic has only performed a total of 12 space flights so far.

Virgin Galactic is targeting a 2028 delivery date for its next-gen mothership, which is expected to look and perform similarly to VMS Eve but will support a greater flight cadence. Until the new mothership is deployed, Virgin Galactic will need to increase flight frequency using its current ship. A major enhancement program for VMS Eve was completed in 2023. Virgin Galactic is also developing a targeted maintenance plan and operations schedule for Eve, which it expects to result in three space missions weekly. The feasibility of this is unknown though, creating significant uncertainty.

Virgin Galactic has also recently started to highlight alternative opportunities for its launch vehicle. An aircraft that can reach high altitudes for long durations while carrying a heavy payload opens up a number of opportunities, including:

  • Airborne R&D testing
  • Intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance support
  • Command and control node capabilities
  • Golden Dome initiatives

VMS Eve is the world’s largest all composite aircraft in service and can transport Virgin Galactic's spaceship to 45,000 feet. While adjacent opportunities could help Virgin Galactic to generate additional growth at some point in the future, I don't believe it will help with the company's current cash crunch.

Virgin Galactic is also conducting a feasibility assessment for a spaceport in Southern Italy. In addition, the company has suggested that the Middle East is a high potential location for a spaceport. I don't think this is particularly important at this stage though, beyond trying to create investor enthusiasm for Virgin Galactic's long-term prospects. If the company can reduce cash burn and transition to profitability, there will obviously be additional spaceports, and some of these will be placed in geographically diverse locations which are potentially useful for point-to-point travel.

Financial Analysis

Virgin Galactic generated roughly $500,000 of revenue in the first quarter from future astronaut access fees and event fees. Second quarter revenue is expected to total roughly $400,000. Revenue is a fairly meaningless indicator given the current state of Virgin Galactic's business though.

Operating expenses totaled $89 million in the first quarter, down 21% YoY. Free cash flow was negative $122 million, with $46 million in CapEx. Cash burn is expected to decline to $105-$115 million in the second quarter, and continue falling as the year progresses. Virgin Galactic expects around half of its spend in 2025 will go towards one-time CapEx (tooling, manufacturing capacity and the production of the first two new Delta Class SpaceShips).

Virgin Galactic had $567 million in cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities at the end of Q1. Approximately $31 million in gross proceeds came from an at-the-market equity offering in the quarter. Virgin Galactic also has over $400 million of debt maturing in early 2027.

The first spaceflight reservations are expected in the first quarter of 2026. While pricing hasn't been set yet, Virgin Galactic has suggested it will be upwards of $600,000. The company also believes that a 1-2 year backlog is appropriate, which suggests several hundred million dollars in cash could be raised.

Assuming additional customer deposits total at least $100 million, Virgin Galactic may have enough cash to begin scaling its operations. The company will need to be on a clear trajectory towards profitability by early 2027 though due to its debt. I question how much money will actually be brought in though, particularly given the doubts surrounding the company's future.

Conclusion

Virgin Galactic is targeting around $450 million in revenue with its initial ships. It is questionable whether Eve will support the necessary operational pace though. Even if Virgin Galactic can rapidly ramp to hundreds of millions of dollars in revenue, the company won't necessarily be profitable straight away either. How quickly Virgin Galactic can ramp operations and reach breakeven is critical given the company's market capitalization and balance sheet.

As the recent share price spike demonstrates, Virgin Galactic only needs to create a mere sense that its business is viable to drive its share price significantly higher. This would make raising capital much easier, potentially creating a positive feedback loop. While I believe that Virgin Galactic is in a difficult situation, and is unlikely to survive longer term, the potential for large price spikes make the company a poor short candidate.

data.title: Banner Corporation: The Upside Isn't Over Yet
data.description:
Way back in September 2023, one company that I came out bullish about was Banner Corporation (NASDAQ:BANR). In case you aren't familiar with the enterprise, it operates a string of bank branch offices and loan production offices that are primarily focused on the western coast of the US. It also has some other operations as well, such as those associated with the mortgage industry. At that time, acknowledged that the enterprise had some shortcomings. This included weaknesses from a deposit perspective. By considering the overall growth of the business from a revenue and profit standpoint, I maintained that it was an attractive prospect worthy of a ‘buy’ rating. From that time through today, the company has achieved some improvement on the balance sheet side of things. When it comes to the income statement, the picture has been admittedly mixed.

Despite these troubles, the high asset quality of the business, combined with how attractively priced shares were back then, ultimately resulted in an upside for shareholders. From the time I rated it a ‘buy’ in September 2023 until the time of this writing, shares are up a whopping 50.8%. That's well above the 31.2% rise that the S&P 500 experienced. Looking at the picture now, the company is certainly not doing badly. The stock is a little more expensive than I would like it to be. But with the asset quality, where it currently is, I believe that maintaining a very soft ‘buy’ rating is appropriate at this moment.

Checking back in with Banner Corporation

In case you aren't familiar with Banner Corporation, the company operates as a bank holding firm that is incorporated in the state of Washington. As of the end of its 2024 fiscal year, the firm boasted 135 branch offices and 13 loan production offices. These were primarily focused on the West Coast, specifically Washington, Oregon, and California. The company does have a presence in other western states like Idaho and Utah. From its locations, it provides customers with a wide array of commercial banking services and financial products. These include traditional bank offerings such as deposit accounts, mortgages, loans for commercial business and commercial real estate activities, land and land development loans, construction loans, agriculture business loans, and even SBA loans.

As I mentioned at the start of this article, Banner Corporation has had some rocky results in recent years. Back in 2023, for instance, the company had deposits on its books of $13.62 billion. Those ended up falling to $13.03 billion in 2023. But since then, deposits have been on the rise. Last year, they ended at $13.51 billion. And in the most recent quarter of this year, they were $13.59 billion. I wanted to see how much of its deposit base was brokered. Many banks will take on costly brokered deposits in order to grow. This is not necessarily a bad thing, but it does mean that growth is more expensive. Fortunately, in the most recent quarter, only $75.3 million of the deposits on the company's books were brokered. This represents just 0.6% of the total amount.

We should also be paying attention to the company's loan portfolio. Unlike deposits, these have actually been rising pretty consistently. The firm went from $10.01 billion in 2022 to $11.20 billion last year. And in the most recent quarter, they increased further to $11.28 billion. The largest chunk of the company's loan portfolio involved commercial real estate. That accounted for a full 33% of the amount. Whenever I see such a large exposure to commercial real estate, I have to wonder how much of the exposure involves office assets. After all, office vacancy rates have been rising in the US and any sort of debt tied to them should be considered riskier than most other types of commercial real estate. The good news is that, according to management, only 5.4% of its total loan portfolio involves these types of assets.

Outside of commercial real estate, the greatest concentration that the company has is in commercial loans. This would be 21% of the total. This was followed up by construction loans at 15%, one to four family mortgage loans at 14%, multifamily loans at 8%, consumer loans at 6%, and agricultural loans at the remaining 3%.

Other aspects of the balance sheet deserve some attention also. Securities would be one example here. In recent years, management has been shifting toward a lower concentration of securities on its books. These have dropped from $3.94 billion in 2022 to $3.10 billion in the most recent quarter. Over the same window of time, cash and cash equivalents have actually increased from $243.1 million to $441.9 million. Meanwhile, debt has been on the decline. After peaking at $665.1 million in 2023, it pulled back to $446.7 million in the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year.

When it comes to the income statement, the picture has not been as robust. Despite the growing balance sheet, the company has seen its net interest income contract from $542.8 million in 2022 to $534.1 million in 2024. This is in spite of the fact that the company has seen a small uptick in its net interest margin from 3.68% to 3.75%. During this time, the company saw a reduction in its provision for credit losses as well. This might all seem paradoxical. But it appears that the primary culprit here was the fact that the enterprise saw more of its deposits shift toward interest-bearing accounts and away from non-interest-bearing accounts. Even as interest-bearing accounts saw an increase from an average balance for each year from $7.83 billion to $8.61 billion, non-interest-bearing deposits shrank from $6.43 billion to $4.65 billion. During times when interest rates are rising, depositors will seek out greater yield. And that is precisely what we had here. The good news is that, for the first quarter of 2025, the net interest income that the company generated was $137.9 million. That was up from $132.4 million and was driven at least in part by a jump in net interest margin from 3.74% to 3.92%.

Non-interest income, meanwhile, has started to improve. After dropping from $75.3 million in 2022 to $44.4 million in 2023, it grew to $66.9 million last year. What's more, the $19.1 million reported in the most recent quarter beat out the $11.6 million reported the previous year rather handily. This recent improvement was driven largely by a $4.9 million loss on the sale of securities last year and by a $1.3 million unfavorable swing associated with the net change in valuation of financial instruments that the company has carried at fair value.

Net income has also been a bit problematic. From 2022 to 2024, it declined from $195.4 million to $168.9 million. But recently, it has started to improve. The strong net interest income and non-interest income readings achieved during the first quarter of this year allowed net profits to grow year over year from $37.6 million to $45.1 million. That's not a huge growth, but it's growth nonetheless.

If we use the historical performance that Banner Corporation achieved last year, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. The price-to-earnings multiple that it has of 12.8 is, admittedly, above the 6 to 10 ranges that I like to see. But in that chart, you can see how the company stacks up against five similar firms. Only one of the five competitors that I compared it to is cheaper than it is on this basis. In the chart below, you can see that the tables turn a bit. This chart shows the price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples. In this case, Banner Corporation is a bit more expensive, with four of the five competitors being cheaper than it is on a price-to-book basis and three of the five being cheaper on a price-to-tangible book basis. Even so, the price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples are not terribly high on an absolute basis.

Normally, I would take this opportunity to downgrade a business like this. But the one thing that has me holding on to a soft ‘buy’ rating is the fact that asset quality is appealing. In the first chart below, you can see the firms' return on assets reading 1.15%. While two of the five firms that I compared it to are higher than it is, the total reading of 1.15% is above the 1% minimum threshold that I would like to see. And in the subsequent chart, you can see the same thing regarding return on equity. The 10.17% reading that we have here is above four of the five competitors and is above the 10% minimum threshold that I like to see.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I must say that I remain optimistic about Banner Corporation. Even though results have been a bit mixed in recent months, the company has high asset quality and shares are not priced at unreasonable levels. It is certainly not an ideal prospect and is not my top pick in this space. But it is attractive enough, in my opinion, to justify a soft ‘buy’ rating at this time.

data.title: Diamond Hill International Strategy Q1 2025 Market Commentary
data.description:
Market Commentary

International equity markets experienced broad-based gains in the first quarter, as the MSCI ACWI ex-USA Index rose by 5.23%. Northern Europe, particularly Germany, posted strong returns driven by optimism about a landmark €1 trillion defense and infrastructure spending package. This unprecedented fiscal initiative represents a marked departure from Germany’s traditional fiscal conservatism. Equity markets embraced the prospect of increased spending in the region, though bond markets reacted cautiously, with yields rising amid concerns about expanding deficits. The long-term impacts of this policy shift remain uncertain, particularly given the varying fiscal conditions across Europe, which may limit the extent to which other nations follow Germany’s path.

Chinese equities also delivered a robust return, propelled by advancements in artificial intelligence. The country’s latest AI innovation, DeepSeek, demonstrated the capability to rival US technologies at significantly lower costs, underscoring the intensifying global AI competition. President Xi Jinping also shifted his stance towards the private sector, publicly emphasizing its vital role in China’s economic growth.

Although fiscal stimulus remained modest and domestic consumption continued to lag, strong export performance provided some balance. However, structural challenges in real estate and demographics persist, and whether recent policy measures can sustainably boost consumer spending remains an open question.

Elsewhere in Asia, equity market returns were mixed. Japan ended the quarter flat, while Australia and India registered modest declines. Australian equities’ performance suffered from the country’s exposure to lower commodity prices, while India’s market faced headwinds from slowing consumer and government spending, compounded by a downturn in IT hiring.

Performance Discussion

Our portfolio generated positive returns during the quarter, although it trailed the robust return of the benchmark. Sector performance was led by strength in consumer discretionary and information technology, while the financials and materials sectors were notable detractors. Geographically, Japan and Switzerland positively contributed to performance, while holdings in the United Kingdom and Germany weighed on relative returns.

Chinese technology company Alibaba (BABA, OTCPK:BABAF) emerged as a significant contributor, benefiting from solid earnings results and increased market optimism around its AI initiatives.

The stock price responded favorably to management’s expanded capital investment to bolster its AI capabilities. President Xi’s renewed support for the private sector also signaled a potential easing in regulatory scrutiny. With Alibaba’s core operations continuing to generate strong cash flows and attractive valuations, we remain optimistic about its prospects.

Sweden-based Spotify (SPOT), a global leader in audio streaming, also contributed positively following strong fourth-quarter and annual results. The company reported continued user growth, alongside notable margin improvements, which exceeded market expectations. Given the stock’s strong appreciation over the past two years, we are managing the position accordingly.

French aerospace leader Safran (OTCPK:SAFRF)(OTCPK:SAFRY) performed strongly, supported by robust demand for aftermarket aerospace products and services. Its position as a leading supplier for narrow-body aircraft engines provides a resilient, recurring revenue stream from maintenance and parts replacement. Recent upticks in aircraft usage and signs of gradual production increases further enhance Safran’s outlook as a durable compounder with sustainable competitive advantages.

Conversely, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM, OTC:TSMWF) detracted from performance despite solid fundamentals.

Geopolitical concerns around Taiwan and broader volatility in the semiconductor cycle negatively influenced investor sentiment. Although near-term pricing pressure and inventory adjustments are standard in the industry, TSMC remains strategically essential to global semiconductor manufacturing, and we continue to view its long-term growth trajectory positively.

Global commodity company Glencore (OTCPK:GLCNF)(OTCPK:GLNCY) underperformed despite stable financial results, reflecting investor concerns about commodity price volatility and uncertainty surrounding Chinese industrial demand. Nonetheless, Glencore’s diversified business model, combining its mining activities and commodity trading operations, provides resilience. We maintain our conviction in Glencore’s long-term positioning in copper and coal and management’s demonstrated ability to allocate capital across various market environments.

UniQure (QURE), a Netherlands-based gene therapy company, also detracted due to regulatory uncertainties stemming from recent changes at the US FDA, potentially slowing drug approvals. While these developments weighed on sentiment, we believe UniQure’s robust platform, advanced manufacturing capabilities and promising pipeline offer considerable long-term value not yet reflected in its share price.

Portfolio Activity

New idea generation was robust during the quarter, and we added eight new positions to the portfolio. There was not one common theme with our additions, as each looks to be an attractive long-term opportunity that stands on its own merits. Concurrently, we exited five positions to redeploy capital into higher-conviction opportunities or because their valuations approached our intrinsic value estimates.

We initiated a position in British American Tobacco (BTI)(OTCPK:BTAFF), a global leader in traditional tobacco products. Despite regulatory headwinds, the company’s robust free cash flow, strong dividend profile and undervalued stake in its Indian subsidiary offer an attractive risk-reward.

Similarly, we added Imperial Brands, another leading global tobacco company distinguished by its strong cash flow generation, which has allowed it to return capital to shareholders aggressively. We don’t believe the current stock price reflects the resilience of its cash flows amidst global uncertainty and the growth of its next-generation tobacco products.

We also established a position in Toyo Suisan Kaisha (OTCPK:TSUKY)(OTCPK:TSUKF), the maker of the popular Maruchan brand of affordable,

staple food products such as ramen noodles. Toyo Suisan’s resilient demand profile across economic cycles, healthy balance sheet, improving capital allocation and expansion opportunities in North American markets offer a unique blend of defensive stability and growth potential.

The North West Company (OTCPK:NNWWF) was another notable addition. As a dominant retailer serving remote and underserved communities in Canada, Alaska and the Caribbean, it benefits from geographic insulation, consistent consumer demand and dependable cash flows, which we believe, as long-term investors, are undervalued in the current stock price.

Melrose Industries (OTCPK:MLSPF), a UK-based aerospace and defense company, was purchased due to the cash flow growth potential from its aerospace Risk and Revenue Sharing Partnerships (RRSPs). These partnerships generate cash flow for Melrose over decade-long periods as engines and parts are serviced for maintenance. Cash flow is typically lower in the early years of the partnership but rises in the later years as more maintenance is needed. Additionally, Melrose’s Structures division is undergoing a multi-year margin expansion plan and investment in additive manufacturing (3D printing) technology, which could enhance profitability.

We initiated a position in Big Yellow Group (OTCPK:BYLOF), the UK’s premier self-storage provider. While the business faces temporary occupancy softness, its strong long-term demand dynamics, robust balance sheet and attractive discount to net asset value (NAV) offer a compelling entry point into one of Europe’s highest-quality real estate businesses.

Boardwalk REIT (OTCPK:BOWFF), a Canadian multi-family residential property operator, was also added. Its strategic positioning in regions with robust employment growth and constrained housing supply supports stable occupancy and rent increases. Coupled with conservative financial management and a significant discount to NAV, we believe Boardwalk represents a compelling opportunity.

Lastly, we added Hitachi (OTCPK:HTHIY)(OTCPK:HTHIF), the Japanese conglomerate, which is executing a multi-year transformation focused on divesting non-core assets and enhancing capital allocation towards higher-margin, growth-oriented businesses in power grids, IT services and consulting. We believe the stock price does not reflect the significant upside potential resulting from these strategic shifts and the growth of its end markets.

On the exit side, we sold Credicorp (BAP), Peru’s largest financial holding company, as the stock reached our estimate of intrinsic value. Julius Baer (OTC:JBPCF), a Switzerland-based global private bank and wealth manager, Ambev (ABEV), the largest brewer in Latin America and GSK (GSK)(OTCPK:GLAXF), a global pharmaceutical company, were all sold to fund higher conviction opportunities elsewhere. Lastly, the Japanese trading house Mitsubishi was sold in favor of other Japanese investments with better long-term business prospects.

Market Outlook

Although international markets started the year strongly, significant uncertainties remain. Growing unpredictability in global trade policy, especially concerning US tariffs and trade relations, poses risks to global supply chains and may pressure companies reliant on international production networks.

Germany’s increased fiscal spending could signal the beginning of a broader capital investment cycle across Europe, but the scale and sustainability of such a shift remain unclear. While markets have initially responded positively to spending announcements, cautious corporate earnings guidance suggests limited near-term visibility and tempered expectations.

We believe capital flows could increasingly shift from US markets towards international equities, driven by compelling valuations and structural improvements. Attractive valuations persist in markets such as Japan, South Korea and many European countries. Additionally, a weakening US dollar, influenced by evolving trade policies and reshoring trends, may further support a shift in performance leadership to international equities.

Our investment strategy remains grounded in disciplined bottom-up analysis. We seek undervalued companies where we identify significant divergence between current stock prices and the business’s intrinsic value over the long term.

Diamond Hill Capital Management, Inc. (DHCM) is an investment adviser registered with the Securities and Exchange Commission and a wholly owned subsidiary of Diamond Hill Investment Group, Inc.; registration does not imply a certain level of skill or training. DHCM provides investment management services to individuals and institutions through mutual funds, separately managed accounts, collective investment trusts, a private fund, a closed-end interval fund and other pooled vehicles including subadvised funds and model delivery programs. A complete list and description of all composites and policies for valuing investments, calculating performance and preparing GIPS reports is available upon request. To receive a complete list and description of all Diamond Hill composites and/or a GIPS® report, contact Scott Stapleton at 614.255.3329, sstapleton@diamond-hill.com or 325 John H. McConnell Blvd., Suite 200, Columbus, OH 43215. A list of broad distribution pooled funds is available upon request. In addition, a list of limited distribution pooled fund descriptions is available upon request. The International Composite is comprised of discretionary, fee-paying, non-wrap accounts managed according to the firm’s International equity strategy. The strategy’s investment objective is to achieve long-term capital appreciation by investing in non-US companies selling for less than our estimate of intrinsic value. The composite typically invests in companies with a market capitalization of $1 billion or greater. The composite results reflect the reinvestment of dividends, capital gains and other earnings when appropriate. Composite returns and benchmark returns are presented gross of withholding taxes on dividends, interest income and capital gains. Returns are calculated using US Dollars. Net returns are calculated by reducing the gross returns by the highest stated fee in the composite fee schedule. Only transaction costs are deducted from gross of fees returns. Prior to 30 September 2022, actual fees were used in calculating net returns. All net returns were changed retroactively to reflect the highest fee in the composite fee schedule. The MSCI ACWI ex USA Index is the benchmark. This index measures the performance of large- and mid-cap stocks in developed (excluding the US) and emerging markets. The index is unmanaged, market capitalization weighted, includes net reinvested dividends, does not reflect fees or expenses (which would lower the return) and is not available for direct investment. Index data source: MSCI, Inc. and Morningstar, Inc. See Diamond Hill - Disclosures for a full copy of the disclaimer. The performance data quoted represents past performance; past performance does not guarantee future results. GIPS® is a registered trademark of CFA Institute. CFA Institute does not endorse or promote this organization, nor does it warrant the accuracy or quality of the content contained herein.

Securities referenced may not be representative of all portfolio holdings. The reader should not assume that an investment in the securities was or will be profitable.
